---
title: "NBA stats and winpct"
author: "Pat Rankin"
format: html
editor: visual
message: False
warning: False
---

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(quarto)

```

# Do NBA teams win more due to 3pt%, TOV, RBS, FT%, or Possessions.

# Create data frame(one predictor variable(win_pct) and 5 independent variables (3Pt%, TOV, RBS, FT%, and pace)

```{r}

teams <- c("DEN","MIA","SAS","CLE","OKC","ATL","MIN","DET","UTA","NYK",
           "CHI","LAL","CHA","PHI","ORL","NOP","POR","HOU","BOS","MEM",
           "GSW","TOR","DAL","LAC","WAS","PHO","IND","SAC","MIL","BKN")

three_pt_pct <- c(39.6, 36.1, 35.9, 36.0, 36.5, 37.1, 37.0, 35.6, 34.5, 37.3,
                   35.6, 35.9, 37.8, 34.9, 34.3, 34.7, 34.3, 36.4, 36.7, 35.3,
                   35.6, 35.4, 31.9, 36.8, 35.9, 34.0, 38.7, 34.0, 36.0, 35.0)

Tov <- c(12.9, 13.7, 13.5, 14.0, 12.6, 14.2, 14.8, 15.1, 15.5 ,13.6,
         15.3, 14.5, 15.4, 13.6, 14.2, 14.2, 17.3, 15.4, 12.4, 15.1,
         15.7, 13.7, 14.5, 14.3 ,15.7, 14.5, 14.5 ,14.3, 15.2, 15.8)

Rbs <- c(44.0, 46.3, 47.0, 44.4, 44.1, 43.5, 44.1, 45.6, 43.8, 45.6,
         45.0, 41.0, 46.1, 43.6, 43.4, 43.9, 46.0, 48.1, 46.4, 42.1,
          42.3, 42.1, 44.7, 40.6, 41.9, 43.1, 41.9, 42.3, 40.7, 39.3)

FTPCT <- c(80.8, 79.2, 78.7, 77.6 ,81.7, 77.4 ,75.2, 76.3, 78.6, 79.2,
           77.6, 76.3, 81.8, 80.9, 80.1, 78.4, 76.5, 76.3, 80.7, 79.3,
           80.7, 76.9, 75.5, 82.3, 76.6, 78.1, 77.6, 77.2, 73.0, 78.0)

pace <- c(102.3, 106.2, 102.8, 102.8, 102.3, 104.7, 103.3, 102.9, 105.7,
          99.67, 105.2, 100.6, 101.0, 103.0, 103.6, 103.8, 104.4, 100.5,
          98.3, 104.5, 102.4, 101.2, 105.2, 99.2, 104.4, 100.6, 103.7,
          101.9, 100.2, 100.1)

win_pct <- c(73.2, 52.4, 75.6, 63.4, 78.0, 56.1, 59.8, 73.2, 26.8, 64.6, 
             37.8, 64.6, 53.7, 54.9, 54.9, 31.7, 51.2, 63.4, 68.3, 30.5,
             45.1, 56.1, 31.7, 51.2, 20.7, 54.9, 23.2, 26.8, 39.0, 24.4)

nba_data <- data.frame(
  teams,
  three_pt_pct,
  Tov,
  Rbs,
  FTPCT,
  pace,
  win_pct
)

nba_data
```

# Perform a linear regression model. Predictor variable is win_pct. The independent variables are three_pt_pct, tov, rbs, FTPCT, and pace.

```{r}
head(nba_data)
model <- lm(win_pct ~ pace + FTPCT + Rbs + Tov + three_pt_pct,
         data = nba_data)
summary(model)
```

**Overall Model Fit**

Your regression model is statistically significant overall:

• 𝐹(5,24) = 6.43

• 𝑝 = 0.0006361, 95% LOC

-   0.0006361 \< 0.05 so the model is statistically significant and you reject the null hypothesis. There is strong statistical evidence that at least one of your independent variables(pace, FT%, Rebounds, Turnovers, or 3PT percentage) is related to win percentage. The relationship I found is unlikely to be due to random chance.

-   The specific variables that matter in terms of whether or not the team wins are Pace(p-value = 0.03000), Rebounds( p = 0.00136), and Turnovers(p = 0.05580) All of these individual p values are less than 0.05. Free throws percentage and three point percentage are the two variables that do do not play a significant part in winning percentage.

This means the predictors, taken together, explain a meaningful amount of variation in team winning percentage.

The model explains:

• Multiple R = 0.5726, which is moderate to fairly strong. This indicates a moderate positive relattionship between the models prediction and actual winning prediction. 0.57 suggests the model has meaninful predictive ability

• Adjusted 𝑅 2 = 0.4835

About 48.35 % of the variation in win percentage is explained by pace, FT%, rebounds, turnovers, and 3PT%.

Your fitted model is:

𝑤𝑖𝑛̂\_𝑝𝑐𝑡 = 1.8738 − 0.0275(𝑃𝑎𝑐𝑒) − 0.0498(𝐹𝑇𝑃𝐶𝑇) + 4.1315(𝑅𝑏𝑠) − 0.0536(𝑇𝑜𝑣)

\+ 1.2766(𝑡ℎ𝑟𝑒𝑒_𝑝𝑡_𝑝𝑐𝑡)

The model collectively explains a meaninful portion of team success, and the relationship is statistically significant.

[**Interpretation of Predictors**]{.underline}

**Pace**

𝛽 = −0.0275, 𝑝 = 0.030

𝑦 = −0.0275𝑥

• Statistically significant at the 5% level.

• Holding other variables constant, a 1-unit increase in pace is associated with about a 0.027 decrease in winning percentage.

• Suggests faster-paced teams in this dataset tended to win slightly less.

The typical prediction error in winning percentage is about 0.12.

[**Key Takeaways**]{.underline}

The most important predictors in your model are:

1\. **Rebounds** → positive effect on winning

2\. **Turnovers** → negative effect on winning

3\. **Pace** → modest negative effect

Meanwhile:

Free throw percentage and three-point percentage were not significant in this sample.

[Rebounds(Rbs)]{.underline}

-   Strongest significant positive predictor

More rebounds are associated with substantially higher winning percentage.

[**Turnovers (Tov)**]{.underline}

-   Significant negative relationship.

-   More turnovers are associated with lower winning percentage.

-   This aligns well with basketball expectations.

[**Free Throw Percentage (FTPCT)**]{.underline}

-   Not statistically significant.

-   Essentially no detectable relationship with winning percentage after controlling for the other variables.

[**Three-Point Percentage (three_pt_pct)**]{.underline}

-   Positive coefficient, but not statistically significant.

-   Suggests 3-point shooting percentage alone may not explain wins once rebounds, turnovers, and pace are included.

-   This indicates the typical prediction error in winning percentage is about 0.12.

-   **Key Takeaways**

The most important predictors in your model are:

1.  **Rebounds** → positive effect on winning

2.  **Turnovers** → negative effect on winning

3.  **Pace** → modest negative effect

Meanwhile:

-   Free throw percentage and three-point percentage were not significant in this sample.

-   The model has moderate explanatory power overall.

Why would the model still be statistically signicant becauise Adjusted R\^2 is far away from 1, although the p value is very low

A low overall model p-value and a moderate/low Adjusted ![](data:image/png;base64,)measure **different things**, so they can absolutely occur together.

**What the Overall F-test p-value Means**

Your model’s p-value:

comes from the overall F-test:

-   Null hypothesis

-   Alternative:\
    At least one predictor has a nonzero relationship with win_pct.

Because the p-value is very small, you reject the null hypothesis and conclude:

The predictors collectively improve prediction beyond a model with no predictors.

That only tells you the model has **real explanatory power**, not that it explains *all* or even *most* of the variance.

**What Adjusted** ![](data:image/png;base64,)**Means**

Your adjusted ![](data:image/png;base64,):

means:

After accounting for the number of predictors, the model explains about 48% of the variability in winning percentage.

That is actually fairly reasonable for real-world sports data.

An ![](data:image/png;base64,)does **not** need to be close to 1 for a model to be statistically significant.

**Why Both Can Happen Together**

These two ideas coexist because:

-   Statistical significance asks:

“Is there evidence of a relationship?”

-   

“How much variation do we explain?”

A model can explain only a moderate amount of variation but still do so very consistently.

**Intuition**

Imagine this:

-   Winning percentage depends on many things not in your model:

    -   defense,

    -   injuries,

    -   coaching,

    -   player availability,

    -   schedule strength,

    -   clutch performance,

    -   chemistry,

    -   etc.

So even a good basketball model may never get near:

But if rebounds and turnovers consistently affect winning, the regression detects that relationship clearly, giving a low p-value.

**Important Point**

A statistically significant model does **not** imply:

-   excellent prediction,

-   high practical importance,

-   or near-perfect fit.

It only means:

The relationship is unlikely to be due to random chance.

In your case:

-   The model is statistically meaningful,

-   but still leaves about half the variability unexplained,

-   which is normal in sports analytics.

#### Is there a relationship between three point percentage and team winning percentage in NBA basketball?

```model <- lm(win_pct ~ three_pt_pct, data = nba_data)
summary(model)



str(nba_data)
names(nba_data)
 

```

```{r}
# Create a scatterplot with the fitted regression line


ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = three_pt_pct, y = win_pct)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE)
```

#### [Summary]{.underline}

Equation of the line is:

y = 1.87376 - 0.02751(Pace) - 0.04981(FTPCT) + 0.04131(Rbs) -b(44) - 0.05359(Tov) +

1.27657(three_pt_pct).

For example during the 25-26 season the Denver Nuggets has the following statistics.

WinPct = .732, Pace = 102.3, FTPCt = .808, Rbs = 44, Tov = 12.9, 3p% = .396

y = 0.659 or 65.9% which is their winning percentage for 25-26 season

Note- The coefficient for the rbs variable should be (0.0413153).

```{r}
nba_data$predicted <- predict(model)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

#### Summary

This above model generates predicted win percentages from the fitted model. It stores then into a new column called "predicted".

```{r}
library(ggrepel)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct, label = teams)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_text_repel() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct, label = teams)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text_repel(max.overlaps = Inf) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    subtitle = "NBA Team Performance Model",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
